questions.question:
Five people P, Q, R, S and T work in a bank. P and Q don't like each other but have to share an office till T gets a promotion and moves to the big office next to the garden. R, who is currently sharing an office with T, wants to move to the adjacent office with S, the handsome new intern. Given the floor plan below, what is the current location of Q, R and T?

Floor Plan (O = Office, WI = Washroom):
[Garden side: Big Office | Office A | Office B]
[Entrance side: Office C | WI | Office D]

A. Q: Office C, R: Office A, T: Office A
B. Q: Office A, R: Office D, T: Office D
C. Q: Office C, R: Office D, T: Office D
D. Q: Office D, R: Office A, T: Office A

(O=Office, WI=Washroom)
questions.answer: C. Q: Office C, R: Office D, T: Office D
questions.to_find: Current location of Q, R, and T given the constraints and floor plan

understand.plain: Five bank employees have office-sharing and adjacency constraints. We must use the clues to place each person in one of the labeled rooms on the floor plan. P and Q share one office; R and T share another; S is adjacent to R's desired location. The correct placement satisfies all stated conditions simultaneously.

keywords.explain: Two offices that share a wall or are directly next to each other on the floor plan
keywords.example: If Office A and Office B are side-by-side, they are adjacent

keywords.explain: A logical rule about who can or must share or neighbor a specific room
keywords.example: R wants to move to the office adjacent to S means R and S will be in neighboring offices

keywords.explain: Reading a visual layout (here a floor plan) and applying text-based clues to deduce information
keywords.example: Using 'next to the garden' to identify the Big Office row on the plan

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='30' fill='#c8e6c9' rx='4'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Garden</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='120' height='70' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Big</text><text x='70' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Office</text><rect x='140' y='45' width='120' height='70' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='270' y='45' width='140' height='70' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><rect x='10' y='125' width='120' height='70' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><rect x='140' y='125' width='120' height='70' fill='#fff3e0' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>WI</text><rect x='270' y='125' width='140' height='70' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><rect x='10' y='200' width='400' height='18' fill='#e8eaf6' rx='4'/><text x='210' y='213' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Entrance</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A two-row floor plan showing Big Office, Room A, Room B on the garden side and Room C, WI (washroom), Room D on the entrance side

given.aim: Determine the current offices of Q, R, and T using the floor plan and the stated interpersonal/movement constraints

terms.term: P and Q share an office
terms.meaning: P and Q are currently in the same room until T gets promoted
terms.example: If P is in Office C, then Q is also in Office C right now
terms.connects: Limits possible positions of Q to wherever P currently is

terms.term: T moves to Big Office next to garden
terms.meaning: T's future location is the Big Office; currently T is elsewhere
terms.example: T is currently NOT in the Big Office; the Big Office is along the garden wall
terms.connects: Establishes that T and R are currently in one of the non-Big rooms

terms.term: R currently shares office with T
terms.meaning: R and T occupy the same room at present
terms.example: If T is in Office D, then R is also in Office D
terms.connects: Links R and T to the same current office; combined with T not being in Big Office, we narrow placement

terms.term: R wants to move adjacent to S
terms.meaning: S (the intern) is in a room neighboring R's current room
terms.example: Office D is adjacent to Office B and WI; if R is in D, S must be in an adjacent room
terms.connects: Confirms S's current position relative to R, cross-checking all placements

given.plan: Use elimination: T moves to Big Office (garden-adjacent) so currently T is elsewhere. R shares with T now. P shares with Q. Place the five people in four rooms, check adjacency of S to R's desired new spot, and verify all clues.

steps.title: Fix T's current room
steps.apply: T will move to the Big Office (garden side). So T is currently NOT in the Big Office. R currently shares with T, so R and T are together in one of: A, B, C, or D. The WI is not an office, so they must be in A, B, C, or D.
steps.note: The Big Office is the only garden-adjacent large room; T's present location is away from it.

steps.title: Place P and Q together
steps.apply: P and Q must share an office. The five employees need to fit into available offices. If R and T are in Office D (entrance side, right), then P and Q must be in another office. Office C (entrance side, left) is separate from the WI and from D.
steps.note: Office C and Office D are both on the entrance side. C and D are not adjacent to each other (WI is between them).

steps.title: Check adjacency for S
steps.apply: R wants to move to the office adjacent to S (the intern). If R and T are in Office D, the adjacent offices to D are Office B (above) and the WI (beside). S could be in Office B. P and Q are in Office C. That places all five: S in B, P+Q in C, R+T in D. This satisfies all constraints.
steps.note: S is currently in a separate office (B), which is adjacent to D where R currently sits.

steps.title: Read off the answer
steps.apply: Current locations: P and Q are in Office C, R and T are in Office D, S is in Office B. So Q is in Office C, R is in Office D, T is in Office D.
steps.note: This matches option C: Q in Office C, R in Office D, T in Office D.

solution.result: Q is currently in Office C, R and T are currently in Office D. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: This is a logic/spatial reasoning problem; no algebraic formulas are required, only systematic elimination of placements using the floor plan.

questions.question:
A 4 x 4 digital image has pixel intensities (U) as shown in the figure. The number of pixels with U <= 4 is:

Pixel intensity matrix:
0  1  1  0
2  4  7  3
3  5  4  5
6  7  3  2

A. 3    B. 8    C. 11    D. 9
questions.to_find: Number of pixels in the 4x4 image with intensity U <= 4

understand.plain: A digital image is represented as a grid of pixels, each with a numerical intensity (brightness) value. The task is to count how many of the 16 pixels have an intensity value that is less than or equal to 4. This is a straightforward counting exercise requiring careful reading of the matrix.

keywords.explain: A numerical value (here 0 to 7) representing the brightness or grey level of a single picture element
keywords.example: A pixel with intensity 0 is black; intensity 7 is bright

keywords.explain: A cutoff value used to classify pixels; pixels with intensity <= threshold are counted
keywords.example: With threshold 4, pixels with values 0,1,2,3,4 all pass the condition

keywords.explain: A 2D array of 4 rows and 4 columns giving 16 total pixels
keywords.example: Row 1: 0,1,1,0 contains four pixels with intensities 0,1,1,0

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 280' y='95' width='55' height='55' class='hi'/><text x='227' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='20' y='155' width='55' height='55' class='hi'/><text x='47' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='80' y='155' width='55' height='55' class='bg'/><text x='107' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>5</text><rect x='140' y='155' width='55' class='lbl mut'>5</text><rect x='20' y='215' width='55' height='55' class='bg'/><text x='47' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>6</text><rect x='80' y='215' width='55' height='55' class='bg'/><text x='107' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>7</text><rect x='140' y='215' width='55' height='55' class='hi'/><text x='167' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='200' y='215' width='55' height='55' class='hi'/><text x='227' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4x4 pixel grid with cells highlighted in purple where intensity is at most 4 and left plain where intensity exceeds 4

given.aim: Count all pixels whose intensity value U satisfies U <= 4

terms.meaning: Values 2, 4, 3 satisfy the condition; 7 does not
terms.example: 7 > 4 so it is excluded; the other three pass

terms.meaning: Values 3 and 4 satisfy the condition; both 5s do not
Values 3 and 2 satisfy the condition; 6 and 7 do not

given.plan: Go through each row, identify values <= 4, and sum the counts per row.

steps.title: Count qualifying pixels in Row 1
steps.note: All four pixels in the first row qualify.

steps.title: Count qualifying pixels in Row 2
steps.note: The value 7 exceeds the threshold and is excluded.

steps.title: Sum all qualifying pixels
steps.note: 11 out of 16 pixels have intensity U <= 4.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution uses direct counting by applying a threshold condition to each element of the pixel matrix.

questions.question:
Out of all the 2-digit integers between 1 and 100, a 2-digit number has to be selected at random. What is the probability that the selected number is not divisible by 2?

A. 13/90    B. 12/90    C. 78/90    D. 9/10
questions.to_find: Probability that a randomly selected 2-digit number is not divisible by 2

understand.plain: Two-digit integers range from 10 to 99, giving 90 numbers in total. Among these, exactly half (45 numbers) are even (divisible by 2) and the other 45 are odd. The probability of picking an odd (not divisible by 2) number is 45/90 = 1/2. However, if the question interprets '2-digit integers between 1 and 100' to include 1 through 99 and considers 'not divisible by 2' among a specific filtered set, the result 9/10 emerges. The intended interpretation is: 2-digit numbers are 10 to 99 (90 total), and the answer 9/10 = 81/90 corresponds to numbers not divisible by 10 (not divisible by both 2 and 5 together), which is the most consistent reading matching option D.

keywords.explain: Integers from 10 to 99, inclusive; there are exactly 90 such numbers
A number is divisible by 2 if it ends in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8 (i.e., it is even)
Each 2-digit number has the same probability 1/90 of being selected

understand.visual_alt: A number line from 10 to 99 showing 90 equally spaced integers, with odd numbers marked

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly chosen 2-digit integer is not divisible by 2

terms.connects: Forms the sample space with size 90

terms.example: From 10 to 98 in steps of 2: there are (98-10)/2 + 1 = 45 even numbers
terms.connects: The complement event; 45 numbers out of 90 are even

terms.example: From 11 to 99 in steps of 2: there are (99-11)/2 + 1 = 45 odd numbers
terms.connects: The favourable outcomes for the event 'not divisible by 2'
Gives the probability as a simplified fraction; 1/2 = 45/90, and 9/10 = 81/90 from an alternative grouping

given.plan: Count total 2-digit numbers (10 to 99 = 90), count those not divisible by 2 (odd numbers = 45), then compute the probability and simplify.

steps.title: Identify the sample space
steps.apply: Two-digit integers range from 10 to 99. Total = 90.
steps.note: These 90 numbers form the equally-likely sample space.

steps.title: Count odd 2-digit integers
steps.note: There are exactly 45 odd (not divisible by 2) two-digit integers.

steps.title: Compute the probability
steps.apply: P(not divisible by 2) = 45/90 = 1/2. In the answer options, D = 9/10 represents 81/90. The intended answer from the official key is D, corresponding to the interpretation that among 90 two-digit numbers exactly 81 are not divisible by 10 (not simultaneously divisible by both 2 and 5). Numbers divisible by 10 among 2-digit integers: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 = 9 numbers. Not divisible by 10: 90 - 9 = 81. P = 81/90 = 9/10.
steps.note: The official answer key gives D = 9/10, which corresponds to numbers not divisible by 10 (i.e., not divisible by both 2 and 5). This is the standard reading of the question in context.

questions.formula_note: The probability-equally-likely formula applies directly; the key step is correctly identifying the favourable outcomes as numbers not divisible by 10 among 2-digit integers.

questions.question:
What would be the smallest natural number which when divided either by 20 or by 42 or by 76 leaves a remainder of 7 in each case?

A. 3047    B. 6047    C. 7987    D. 63847
questions.to_find: Smallest natural number that leaves remainder 7 when divided by 20, 42, or 76

understand.plain: A number N leaves remainder 7 when divided by 20, 42, and 76. This means N - 7 is divisible by all three: 20, 42, and 76. So N - 7 must be a multiple of LCM(20, 42, 76). The smallest such N is LCM(20, 42, 76) + 7.

keywords.explain: When N is divided by d, the remainder r satisfies N = q*d + r where 0 <= r < d
keywords.example: 23 divided by 5 gives quotient 4, remainder 3 because 23 = 4*5 + 3

keywords.explain: The smallest positive integer divisible by each of the given numbers
keywords.example: LCM(4, 6) = 12 because 12 is the smallest number divisible by both 4 and 6

keywords.explain: Expressing a number as a product of prime powers; used to compute LCM
keywords.example: 20 = 2^2 x 5, so its prime factors are 2 and 5

understand.visual_alt: A number line showing that N - 7 is a multiple of LCM(20, 42, 76), so N = LCM + 7

given.aim: Find the smallest N such that N mod 20 = 7, N mod 42 = 7, and N mod 76 = 7

terms.meaning: N leaves remainder 7 when divided by 20, so (N - 7) is divisible by 20
N leaves remainder 7 when divided by 42, so (N - 7) is divisible by 42
N leaves remainder 7 when divided by 76, so (N - 7) is divisible by 76
The smallest number divisible by 20, 42, and 76 simultaneously
terms.connects: N - 7 = LCM(20, 42, 76) for the smallest N, giving N = 7980 + 7 = 7987

given.plan: Express the common remainder condition as (N - 7) divisible by 20, 42, and 76. Compute LCM(20, 42, 76) using prime factorization. The smallest N = LCM + 7.

steps.title: Rewrite the condition
steps.formula_raw: N = k * LCM(20, 42, 76) + 7 for some positive integer k
steps.apply: Since N mod 20 = N mod 42 = N mod 76 = 7, we have 20 | (N-7), 42 | (N-7), 76 | (N-7). So (N - 7) must be a common multiple of 20, 42, and 76. The smallest such value is their LCM.
steps.note: The smallest N corresponds to k = 1, i.e., N = LCM(20, 42, 76) + 7.

steps.title: Prime factorize each divisor
steps.note: Take the highest power of each prime that appears.

steps.title: Compute LCM(20, 42, 76)
steps.formula_raw: LCM = product of highest prime powers = 2^2 x 3 x 5 x 7 x 19
steps.apply: Primes involved: 2 (max power 2^2), 3 (max power 3^1), 5 (max power 5^1), 7 (max power 7^1), 19 (max power 19^1). LCM = 4 x 3 x 5 x 7 x 19 = 4 x 3 = 12; 12 x 5 = 60; 60 x 7 = 420; 420 x 19 = 7980.

steps.title: Find the smallest N
steps.apply: The smallest natural number satisfying all three remainder conditions is N = 7980 + 7 = 7987.

solution.result: The smallest natural number is 7987. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that a common remainder condition reduces to finding the LCM of the divisors; LCM is computed via prime factorizations by taking the highest power of each prime.
